paper_map.title: Formation of precipitates and their effects on the mechanical properties in a Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steel
paper_map.abstract_summary: This study investigates the effects of Ti and V additions on κ-carbide precipitation in Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels. The formation of (Ti,V)C carbides as carbon sinks suppresses homogeneous κ-carbide precipitation and promotes heterogeneous precipitation at (Ti,V)C edges. This modified distribution preserves work-hardening rate and increases tensile strength from 1004 to 1096 MPa. TEM reveals planar slip deformation with narrower slip band spacing in Ti/V-modified steel.
paper_map.research_objective: To address the trade-off between yield strength and work-hardening capacity caused by uniformly distributed κ-carbides by introducing strong carbide-forming elements Ti and V to achieve heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation.
key_sections: Introduction: Background on κ-carbide precipitation, work-hardening trade-off, and proposed Ti/V microalloying strategy
Experiments: Steel preparation, heat treatment, microstructural characterization (TEM, etc.), mechanical testing
Results: Microstructure characteristics (grain size, precipitate size/distribution) and mechanical properties (strength, work-hardening)
Discussion: κ-carbide precipitation mechanisms, strengthening mechanisms (Orowan, coherency), work-hardening characteristics and slip band evolution
Conclusion: Summary of key findings on heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation and improved mechanical properties
